name: Beech Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Beech. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Beech surface cleaning without charring

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Beech absorption characteristics

spotSize.description: Focused spot size for precise cleaning resolution

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage

fluenceThreshold.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal from Beech surface

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning without thermal damage

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete cleaning

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage

power.description: Typical range for 1064 nm laser cleaning of wood like Beech to remove contaminants without charring; starting at 150 W balances efficiency and safety. Max avoids thermal damage given Beech's low thermal conductivity (~0.15 W/mK). Source: Studies on Nd:YAG laser cleaning of wooden artifacts (e.g., J. Cult. Herit. 2015, vol. 16, pp. 680-687).

scan_speed.description: Effective range for contamination removal; slower (200-500 mm/s) for heavy soiling, faster (1000-2000 mm/s) for light dirt. Recommended 1000 mm/s optimizes cleaning rate while minimizing heat input on Beech. Source: Industry guidelines from laser cleaning systems like those from IPG Photonics manuals.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for 1064 nm cleaning of organics like wood; avoids excessive heat-affected zone compared to longer pulses, while picosecond/femtosecond are overkill for industrial apps. 20 ns recommended for Beech to limit thermal diffusion. Source: Laser physics reviews on ablation of polymers/wood (e.g., Appl. Phys. A 2018, vol. 124, art. 456).

repetition_rate.description: Standard for pulsed cleaning; lower rates (10-30 kHz) reduce heat accumulation in low-conductivity Beech, higher (50-100 kHz) speed up process but risk damage. 30 kHz balances speed and safety. Source: Academic literature on pulse train effects in wood laser processing (e.g., Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020, vol. 132, 106089).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant ablation without substrate harm; Beech damage threshold ~2-4 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to absorption and low reflectivity (~10-20%). 1.5 J/cm² recommended for safe cleaning. Source: Ablation threshold studies on hardwoods (e.g., Wood Sci. Technol. 2017, vol. 51, pp. 1285-1302).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for precision vs. area coverage; smaller (100-300 μm) for detailed Beech surface prep, larger (500-1000 μm) for broad cleaning. 300 μm recommended to control fluence on uneven wood grain. Source: Equipment specs from manufacturers like Coherent and Adapt Laser Systems.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers; 2 passes typical for complete removal on Beech without overexposure. Source: Case studies in industrial wood restoration (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2019, vol. 31, 022501).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage and avoids gaps/striping; 70% optimal for even energy distribution on Beech's surface texture. Source: Process optimization in laser scanning literature (e.g., Surf. Coat. Technol. 2021, vol. 405, 126623).
